name: CNH Industrial N.V.

profile.description: CNH Industrial N.V. designs, manufactures, and sells agricultural and construction equipment worldwide through its Case IH, New Holland, STEYR, and Raven brands. The company operates through Agriculture, Construction, and Financial Services segments, offering tractors, harvesters, precision agriculture solutions, excavators, loaders, and equipment financing. Founded in 1842 (as J.I. Case), headquartered in Basildon, United Kingdom, with approximately 34,000 employees globally.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (1/3): CNH completed the Raven Industries acquisition in 2021 to gain autonomous technology capabilities and acquired Bennamann in 2023 for methane capture technology, though the Bennamann R&D assets were subsequently impaired by $49M in Q3 2025 [1][5]. The 2026 refreshed equipment lineup under Case IH and New Holland represents incremental product updates rather than transformative launches [3]. AI Deployment (1/2): CNH launched its 'AI Tech Assistant' to hundreds of agricultural and construction dealer groups in early 2025 to diagnose machinery issues and propose repair plans [3]. Every member of CNH's global leadership team is running at least one generative or agentic AI pilot program [3]. However, no quantified revenue or cost savings from AI have been reported. Market Share (1/2): CNH is the global #2 agricultural equipment manufacturer behind Deere & Company, with meaningful positions in both ag and construction. However, the company has not demonstrated share gains — Q1 2026 agriculture net sales were essentially flat at $2.6B while the broader ag market continued to contract [2][4]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Green-on-Green targeted spraying technology launching in North America in 2027 with up to 80% herbicide reduction [3]. Goal to make 90% of precision technologies in-house by 2030, up from 80% currently [3]. Timelines are specific but revenue impact remains uncertain. R&D Intensity (1/1): R&D spending at 5.4-8.6% of net sales in 2025, with $1.2B invested in R&D in 2024 [5][6]. This is well above the agricultural equipment industry average of 3-4%, reflecting genuine commitment to precision agriculture and autonomous technology development.

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): CNH's stated mission is 'to provide innovative solutions that empower our customers to efficiently and sustainably produce the food, fuel, and infrastructure that the world needs' [7]. This is clear and identifies the company's purpose — feeding the world through agricultural technology — but is not distinctive enough to differentiate from peers like Deere or AGCO. The mission identifies what CNH does and for whom but lacks the deeper idealistic vision that would earn a higher score. Community Impact (1/2): The CNH Industrial Foundation operates structured charitable programs across three pillars — Civic & Community Empowerment, Food Security, and Disaster Relief — including a $250K grant to Team Rubicon for disaster response [8]. The foundation is organized and active but does not represent outsized or distinctive community investment relative to CNH's scale. Employee Retention (0/2): CEO turnover in 2024 (Scott Wine departed after approximately 3 years, replaced by Gerrit Marx returning from Iveco Group) signals leadership instability [9]. No public data on average employee tenure, voluntary turnover rates, or cultural surveys was found. The UAW labor negotiations at the Racine, Wisconsin plant and a prior extended strike suggest labor-relations friction [10]. Insider Buying (0/2): Net insider selling of approximately $5.08M over the past 12 months. While board chair Suzanne Heywood purchased 52,520 shares and director Howard Buffett purchased 10,100 shares in November 2025, earlier selling by CFO Oddone Incisa ($285K) and multiple other insiders dominated the net position [11].

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on Growth sleeve metrics. CNH's financial profile reflects a capital-intensive industrial company in a cyclical downturn. D/E of 333.7% is above the 300% floor (score 0.0), driven by the Financial Services segment's leverage — total debt of $26.3B against $1.3B cash [1][2]. ROE of 4.8% is well below the 15% target, reflecting the profit collapse — FY2025 net income of $505M was down sharply from prior years [1]. Revenue declined from $24.7B in 2023 to $18.1B in 2025, producing a negative 3-year CAGR of -8.4% (below the -5% floor, score 0.0) [1]. Q1 2026 revenue was flat at $3.83B with net income of just $10M and EPS of $0.01 [2]. Bright spots: Forward P/E of 14.5 is well below the 20 target (score 1.0), reflecting depressed earnings expectations with recovery priced in. Forward PEG of 0.6 is excellent at below 1.0 (score 1.0), suggesting the market prices significant earnings recovery. Profit margin of 2.1% is severely compressed from historical levels. Liquidity is strong with 15.4M average daily volume at $10.60 ($163M daily liquidity).

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): CNH's core business — designing, manufacturing, and distributing physical agricultural and construction equipment — is structurally immune to AI displacement. Tractors, combines, excavators, and loaders are physical-world products that AI cannot substitute [3]. The global installed base of farm equipment requires physical maintenance, parts, and dealer networks that no AI model can replicate. Moreover, AI is a clear tailwind for CNH's precision agriculture business — autonomous driving, targeted spraying (60-80% herbicide reduction), AI-assisted crop management, and predictive maintenance all increase the value proposition of CNH's equipment [3]. The Financial Services segment provides equipment financing that requires physical collateral and regulatory compliance, further insulating it from AI disruption. No analysts have flagged AI as a substitution risk for CNH; the consensus is that AI enhances rather than threatens agricultural equipment demand. AI Opportunity (1/1): CNH is actively generating value from AI deployment. The AI Tech Assistant launched in early 2025 is shipping to hundreds of dealer groups, providing real-time machinery diagnostics [3]. Precision agriculture technologies — including AI-driven planter accuracy (95% seed placement within 0-5cm) and generative AI for crop management insights connecting seeds, fertilizers, equipment, and weather data — represent tangible AI-enabled product differentiation [3]. While AI-specific revenue is not separately disclosed, precision agriculture technology is a growing share of equipment value.

infinite_game.summary: CNH Industrial demonstrates a mixed infinite game posture. The company has a genuine connection to a long-term cause — feeding the world through agricultural innovation — rooted in a heritage dating to 1842. However, leadership instability (CEO turnover in 2024), labor friction (UAW negotiations), and a cyclical business model that drives aggressive cost-cutting during downturns create tension with fully infinite thinking. The 'Iron + Tech' strategic pivot toward precision agriculture during a severe industry downturn shows some courage, but the dividend cut from $0.47 to $0.10 per share signals prioritization of financial survival over stakeholder returns.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): CNH's purpose of empowering customers to produce the food, fuel, and infrastructure the world needs connects the company's work to a genuinely important cause — global food security. The heritage traces to J.I. Case's founding in 1842, giving the company nearly two centuries of agricultural equipment history [7]. CEO Gerrit Marx has stated that 'Agriculture's biggest challenge is to feed more people, with less land, under increasingly difficult conditions,' which articulates a meaningful challenge [3]. However, this cause has not been validated through consistent purpose-driven strategic decisions over decades in the way Sinek requires for top scores. The CEO turnover (Wine to Marx in 2024), the corporate restructuring history (Fiat Industrial merger, Iveco spin-off), and the aggressive financial engineering suggest the cause is genuine but not yet the company's competitive advantage.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): CNH has approximately 34,000 employees across global operations with generally stable workforce levels. However, the UAW labor negotiations at the Racine, Wisconsin plant — following a prior extended strike — indicate meaningful labor-management friction [10]. The CEO transition in mid-2024 (Scott Wine departing after 3 years, Gerrit Marx returning from Iveco) created organizational uncertainty, though Marx's prior CNH experience provided some continuity [9]. No evidence of exceptional trust, empowerment, or cultural distinctiveness was found. The cyclical nature of the business necessitates periodic workforce reductions during downturns, which inherently limits the Circle of Safety.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): CNH competes directly with Deere & Company and AGCO Corporation, and management's competitive language focuses on 'product leadership' and 'customer-first' rather than market share warfare or competitor destruction [3]. CEO Marx's strategic emphasis on precision agriculture technology and the 'Iron + Tech' transformation reflects a focus on self-improvement rather than beating specific rivals. CNH openly acknowledges competitors' strengths in precision agriculture while charting its own path toward 90% in-house precision technology by 2030 [3].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): CNH has evolved its business model over recent years through meaningful but incremental shifts. The Raven Industries acquisition (2021) brought autonomous technology capabilities, and the company is pivoting toward an 'Iron + Tech' model that integrates precision agriculture, AI, and autonomous technology into its equipment platform [3]. The target of making 90% of precision technologies in-house by 2030 represents genuine strategic evolution. However, this is not a transformative reinvention — CNH remains fundamentally an agricultural and construction equipment manufacturer. The company has not abandoned a core business or made a bet that redefined its identity.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): CNH has demonstrated some courage by increasing R&D investment during a severe agricultural downturn — R&D spending reached 5.4-8.6% of net sales in 2025 even as revenue declined 9% and net income collapsed [1][5]. The 'Iron + Tech' strategic pivot toward precision agriculture represents a meaningful long-term bet made during industry weakness. Marx designated agriculture as the 'core business' shortly after taking over, signaling strategic clarity [9]. However, the aggressive dividend cut from $0.47 to $0.10 per share and the guidance for flat-to-declining sales in 2026 suggest the company is managing for survival rather than making costly bets in service of a cause.

investment_thesis: CNH Industrial trades at a compelling forward valuation — 14.5x forward earnings and a 0.6x PEG ratio — reflecting severely depressed current earnings that the market expects to recover. Q1 2026 revenue of $3.83B was flat year-over-year with net income of just $10M ($0.01 EPS), as higher tariffs, lower shipment volumes, and increased R&D spending compressed margins to 2.5-3.5% guided EBIT margins for FY2026 [1][2]. The company's strategic pivot to 'Iron + Tech' — targeting 90% in-house precision agriculture by 2030, deploying AI-powered dealer tools, and launching Green-on-Green targeted spraying technology in 2027 — positions CNH to capture value from the digitization of agriculture [3]. The agricultural downcycle (global ag equipment demand expected to decline a further 5% in 2026) creates a potential trough entry point if the cycle turns, with significant operating leverage to the upside given the current near-breakeven profitability [1][2].
thesis_continued: The bear case is substantial. CNH's balance sheet carries $26.3B in total debt against just $1.3B cash, producing a D/E ratio of 334% that reflects the capital-intensive Financial Services segment but still constrains strategic flexibility during a prolonged downturn [1]. The dividend has been slashed from $0.47/share in 2024 to $0.10 in 2026, signaling management's concern about cash preservation [12]. Revenue has declined from $24.7B (2023) to $18.1B (2025), a 27% contraction over two years, and FY2026 guidance for flat-to-down-4% industrial sales suggests no recovery this year [1][2]. Construction segment Adjusted EBIT turned to a $28M loss in Q1 2026 [2]. CEO Gerrit Marx has been in the role for less than two years following Scott Wine's abrupt departure, creating execution risk during a critical transformation [9]. The UAW labor negotiations at Racine add operational uncertainty [10]. At $10.60/share, the stock is pricing in a recovery that has not yet materialized — if the ag downcycle extends into 2027, the current valuation may not provide adequate margin of safety.
key_catalysts: Agricultural equipment cycle recovery — global ag demand has declined for two consecutive years and dealer inventories are normalizing, creating conditions for a restock cycle that could drive significant operating leverage from CNH's depressed 2-3% margins back toward historical 8-10% levels

Precision agriculture technology monetization — Green-on-Green targeted spraying (80% herbicide reduction) launching in North America 2027, AI Tech Assistant expanding across dealer networks, and the 90% in-house precision tech target by 2030 create a higher-margin recurring technology revenue stream [3]

Forward PEG of 0.6x implies substantial earnings recovery is already expected — if consensus estimates prove conservative and EPS recovers toward the $0.73 forward estimate, the stock could re-rate significantly from the current $10.60 level
key_risks: Severe balance sheet leverage — $26.3B total debt with D/E of 334% constrains financial flexibility during a prolonged ag downcycle; a further revenue decline could pressure debt covenants in the Financial Services segment and force additional dividend cuts or asset sales [1]

Agricultural downcycle extension risk — management expects global ag equipment demand to decline a further 5% in 2026, and tariff escalation or commodity price weakness could push the trough into 2027, delaying the earnings recovery the valuation depends on [2]

Execution risk from leadership transition and labor friction — CEO Marx has been in role less than two years following Wine's abrupt departure, the 'Iron + Tech' pivot requires sustained R&D investment during a period of severely compressed profitability, and UAW negotiations at the Racine plant could escalate to a production-disrupting strike [9][10]
